cards.text: std::move is defined in the {{c1::<utility>}} header.
std::move {{c1::casts}} an l-value to an {{c2::r-value reference}}, allowing it to be moved rather than copied.

cards.front: What does std::move actually do to the object you pass it?
cards.back: Nothing! std::move doesn't move anything. It only casts the object to an r-value reference. The actual move happens when that r-value is used (e.g., in a move constructor).

cards.question: How do you move an l-value into a function or variable?
cards.explanation: std::move casts the l-value str1 to an r-value reference, enabling move semantics.

Use std::move when:
  • You want to transfer ownership of an l-value's resources
  • You're done using an object and want to move it into a container/function
  • Implementing move operations
Don't use when:
  • On const objects (move disabled)
  • You still need to use the object
examples: // str is now in moved-from state, don't use it

cards.question: How can you implement swap using std::move?
cards.explanation: Using std::move for swap is more efficient than copying, especially for expensive-to-copy types like std::string or std::vector.

cards.text: Calling std::move on a {{c1::const}} object will invoke the {{c2::copy}} constructor/assignment, not the move version, because const r-value references can bind to const l-value references.

cards.front: What can you safely do with an object after calling std::move on it?
cards.back:
You can:
  • Destroy it (call destructor)
  • Assign a new value to it
You should NOT:
  • Use its value
  • Call methods that depend on its value
It's in a valid but unspecified state.

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Using str after it's been moved from is undefined behavior. str is in a valid but unspecified state.
cards.explanation: After std::move(str), the string has likely been moved, so accessing it may print empty string or garbage. Don't use objects after moving from them.

cards.text: Don't use std::move on {{c1::return values}} - it can prevent {{c2::Return Value Optimization (RVO)}} and actually make code slower.

cards.question: Why should you avoid std::move here?
cards.answer: Don't use std::move on return values. The compiler already treats the return value as an r-value, and std::move prevents RVO optimization.
cards.explanation: Correct: return str; (no std::move). The compiler will automatically move or optimize away the copy.

cards.front: What's the difference between std::move and std::forward?
cards.back:
std::move unconditionally casts to an r-value reference.
std::forward conditionally casts based on template argument, preserving the value category (used in perfect forwarding).
